Q1: Honestly, my study routine was all over the place this semester. I'm 28, working part-time, and juggling way too many online courses at once—fifteen total, which looking back was insane. I'd try to block out time on weekends and a few evenings, maybe averaging 16 hours a week across everything, but it never felt like enough. I'd read through materials, take notes in my own documents, but the actual structured routine I planned kept falling apart when life got in the way.
Q2: My pattern varied wildly depending on what was due and what crisis was happening that week. I'd have these bursts of intense activity right before deadlines, then periods where I couldn't bring myself to look at anything. Some weeks I was super consistent, others I barely touched my coursework. The anxiety made it hard to stick to any regular schedule—I'd feel paralyzed sometimes even when I knew I should be studying.
Q3: Usually panic, if I'm being honest. I'd get an email notification about a deadline or see something in my calendar and suddenly realize I needed to check what was due. Sometimes I'd log in just to reassure myself I hadn't missed something critical. Other times it was genuine curiosity about discussion posts or wanting to see if there was feedback on something I'd submitted.
Q4: There were brief stretches where logging in became automatic, usually when I was really engaged with a particular topic or discussion thread. I'd check in almost daily during those times, participating in peer discussions and watching videos. But those periods never lasted long—something would disrupt the momentum, whether it was work stress or just feeling overwhelmed by the sheer volume of courses I was taking.
Q5: The discussion forums were actually really valuable to me—I posted regularly and had a lot of peer interactions. Reading other students' perspectives helped concepts click in ways the readings alone didn't. The video lectures were useful too; I watched a lot of them, over 117 minutes worth. I learn best through reading and writing, so anything text-based or that let me engage through writing felt most natural.
Q6: I definitely ignored or delayed a lot of the assignments, which I know hurt me badly. My completion rate was terrible—like 16% or something embarrassing. It wasn't that I thought they were useless; I just kept putting them off because of anxiety and then they'd pile up until it felt impossible to catch up. Some supplementary readings I'd skim or skip entirely when time was tight.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see how it connected to understanding the bigger picture or when it sparked genuine interest. The peer discussions felt worthwhile because I was learning from others, not just passively consuming content. If something felt like busywork or I couldn't see the point, my motivation to engage dropped significantly, especially when I was already stretched thin.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford, honestly. With fifteen courses and limited time, required work always took priority—even though I often didn't complete that either. The optional stuff sometimes looked interesting, but there was always this guilt about not having finished the mandatory assignments first, so I'd skip them. Required activities carried this weight of obligation that optional ones just didn't have.
Q9: Absolutely, a huge amount of my studying happened offline. I'd download PDFs and readings, then work through them on my tablet during my commute or at work during breaks. I took extensive notes in my own Word documents and notebooks. A lot of my actual learning happened away from the LMS—reviewing my notes, re-reading downloaded materials, even just thinking through concepts while doing other things.
Q10: There were many times I was deeply engaged with course material but the LMS would show nothing. I'd spend hours reading downloaded articles, making handwritten notes, or working through problems on paper. Sometimes I'd watch a video once, then pause it and spend an hour processing what I'd learned without any further clicks. My offline study time probably exceeded my online time significantly.
Q11: All the time, yes. I'd download a reading or watch a video once, then return to those materials repeatedly offline over days or weeks. The LMS would only show that single initial access, but I might have reviewed that same content five or six times in my notes or downloaded files. This was especially true for complex topics where I needed multiple passes to really understand.
Q12: There were definitely low points, particularly mid-semester when everything seemed to pile up at once. My anxiety would spike and I'd feel paralyzed—I'd know I needed to work but couldn't bring myself to start. The CBT sessions I attended helped me recognize these patterns, but in the moment, it was hard. Work pressures and just the overwhelming volume of fifteen courses made it feel impossible sometimes.
Q13: Massively. Cognitive overload was real—trying to track fifteen different courses meant I was constantly confused about what was due where. Fatigue from work and the mental load of everything made me less efficient when I did log in. I'd sometimes just scroll through the LMS without really absorbing anything because I was too tired to engage meaningfully. Competing responsibilities always won over coursework.
Q14: Many times. I'd log in with good intentions, open a video or reading, and realize twenty minutes later I'd absorbed nothing. My mind would be elsewhere—worrying about other deadlines, work issues, or just spiraling into anxiety about how behind I was. The activity was there in the logs, but meaningful engagement wasn't. I was going through motions without actually learning.
Q15: Constantly, and it's my biggest regret. I'd avoid assignments because the anxiety around them was so intense—the longer I waited, the worse it got. I knew they were important, knew my grade depended on them, but there was this mental block. Sometimes I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow when I felt better, but tomorrow never came. The avoidance became its own source of stress.
Q16: Deadlines were both motivating and paralyzing. They'd push me into last-minute bursts of activity—I'd suddenly log in frantically, try to complete things, engage with whatever I could. But when deadlines passed and I'd missed them, I'd sometimes just shut down and avoid the course entirely for a while. The deadline pressure created a lot of my LMS activity but also a lot of my anxiety.
Q17: When I got feedback, it actually motivated me to engage more, at least temporarily. Positive feedback especially made me feel like maybe I could do this. But feedback was inconsistent, and when assignments went without response, I felt disconnected. The feedback on assignments I did complete was decent—my scores were actually pretty good when I submitted work. That was frustrating because it showed I could do it if I just followed through.
Q18: The course structure felt overwhelming given my situation. Having so many components across fifteen courses meant I was always playing catch-up. The attendance expectations were impossible for me to meet with my schedule. I think if I'd taken fewer courses, the structure would have worked better, but as it was, I was drowning and the structure felt like it was designed for someone with more time than I had.
Q19: The discussion forums being so active actually made engagement easier—I felt part of a community there. But the rigid assignment deadlines without flexibility made things harder. Once I fell behind, there was no way to catch up, which killed my motivation. Having everything online was convenient for my lifestyle, but the lack of face-to-face accountability meant it was too easy to disappear when things got hard.
Q20: That describes me pretty well, actually. I was highly engaged in terms of logging in, watching videos, participating in discussions—but my assignment submission was terrible. I think it's because engaging with content feels safe and interesting, but assignments carry judgment and the possibility of failure. The anxiety around being evaluated can be paralyzing even when you're genuinely interested in learning the material.
Q21: I can see how that happens—if someone is efficient, studies offline, and just logs in to submit polished work, their activity looks minimal but their performance is strong. That wasn't me though. I had high activity because I was anxious and kept checking things, but my actual productive output was low. Some people just don't need to be visibly active to be learning effectively.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when you're active, what you click on, how long you're technically logged in. But they miss the quality of that engagement entirely. They can't tell if I'm actually focused or just have a tab open while I'm distracted. They definitely miss all the offline learning, the thinking time, the anxiety that might be behind certain patterns. Numbers without context are misleading.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and actively working to understand it, not just going through motions. It's when you're curious, when you think about the material outside of class time, when you want to discuss it with others. For me, engagement happened in bursts—I'd be deeply invested in certain discussions or topics, then completely disengaged when overwhelmed. True engagement is mental and emotional, not just behavioral.
Q24: The LMS can't show how much I actually cared about learning or how hard I was fighting against my own anxiety to participate at all. It can't show the hours I spent studying offline, the genuine interest I had in discussions, or the shame I felt about falling behind. It definitely can't show that my poor performance wasn't from lack of effort or intelligence, but from taking on too much and struggling with mental health. The data tells a story, but it's not the whole story.
